coredata.dc:title: Resolving variable cell viability-induced false negative: Accurate and high-contrast fluorescence diagnosis of cancer enabled by dual organelle targeting and multiple microenvironmental parameters responsive versatile carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Zhaofan
Liang, Zengqiang
Guo, Shuo
Xiao, Yue
Sun, Yuangqiang
Qu, Lingbo
Li, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: Developing effective fluorescence-based diagnostic methods for cancer is of great importance but remains a major challenge owing to the low contrast and limited accuracy induced by the variable cell viability. To increase contrast and accuracy in cancer diagnosis, a dual-organelle-targeting and multiple-parameter-responsive design strategy is proposed here. As a proof of concept, versatile carbon dots (CDs) targeting mitochondria and nucleoli with ability to respond to three environmental parameters (mitochondrial membrane potential, viscosity, and RNA) are developed. These capabilities not only enable high-contrast and accurate imaging of cancer cells but also make the CDs highly robust against interference from environmental parameters alterations induced by cell damage or apoptosis. This new design strategy involving targeting of multiple organelles and multiple responsiveness to environmental parameters could inspire further research in bioactive material-based cancer imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cancer diagnosis
Mitochondria
RNA
Microenvironment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Imaging of cancer cells and normal cells
Imaging of cancer cells after CCCP and LPS stimulation
Imaging of HeLa cells after fixed and DNase, RNase treated
Design, synthesis and characterization of CDs
High contrast imaging of cancer and normal cells
Multi-parameter response leads to higher accuracy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of CDs and particle size distribution. The small image in the lower right corner is an HRTEM image of a single CD. (b) FT-IR spectrum of CDs. (c) Broad XPS spectrum of CD. High-resolution fine spectra of C 1s(d), N 1s(e), and O 1s(f) peaks of the CDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs (10 µg mL−1) with the addition of 0–1000 µg mL−1 RNA. Inset: Relationship between maximum fluorescence emission and RNA concentration. (b) Fluorescence spectra of CDs (10 µg mL−1) in pure PBS, in PBS with RNA (200 µg mL−1), in PBS with RNA (200 µg mL−1) and RNA hydrolase (100 µg mL−1), and in PBS with RNA hydrolase (100 µg mL−1). (c) Fluorescence spectra of CDs (10 µg mL−1) in glycerol/aqueous solutions with different volume ratios. Inset: Linear correlation between the maximum fluorescence intensity of the CDs (10 µg mL−1) and the viscosity (0.893–945 cP) of the solution. (d) Fluorescence intensity of CDs (10 µg mL−1) under interference by 1 mM cations and anions, ROS, RSS, and amino acids. 1: CDs; 2: K+; 3: Ca2+; 4: Na+; 5: Mg2+; 6: Ba2+; 7: Fe2+; 8: Fe3+; 9: Cu2+; 10: Zn2+; 11: HSO3 -; 12: H2PO4 -; 13: HCO3 -; 14: NO2 -; 15:H2O2; 16: OH; 17: HClO; 18: Cys; 19: Hcy; 20: GSH; 21: Tyr; 22: Ser; 23: Glu; 24: Leu; 25: Gln; 26: Asp; 27: Val; 28: Pro; 29: Thr; 30: Lys; 31: Arg; 32: Meth; 33: His; 34: DNA (200 µg mL−1); 35: RNA (200 µg mL−1). λex= 448 nm, λem= 460–700 nm.

Fluorescence images of HeLa cells incubated with CDs (40 µg mL−1) for 1 h, with Hoechst 33342 and MTDR (Mito Tracker™ Deep Red) for 30 min (a) Yellow fluorescence channel image of CDs (λex/λem of 488 nm/510–620 nm). (b) Red fluorescence channel image of MTDR (λex/λem of 638 nm/650–700 nm). (c) Blue fluorescence channel image of Hoechst 33342 (λex/λem of 405 nm/425–470 nm). (d) Merged image of the above three channels. (e) Merged image of CDs and MTDR channels. (f) Merged image of CDs and Hoechst-33342 channels. Scale bars: 25 µm. (g) Distribution diagram of fluorescence intensity along the white line in (d).

(a) Schematic of fluorescence intensity at different sites within the cell. (b) Fluorescence images of different types of normal cells and cancer cells after incubation with CDs (40 µg mL−1) for 1 h (λex/λem of 488 nm/510–620 nm). Scale bars: 25 µm. (c) Separate and combined fluorescence intensities of mitochondria and nucleoli calculated by confocal software for different cells incubated with CDs (40 µg mL−1). (d) Fluorescence images obtained after incubation with CDs (40 µg mL−1) for 1 h of HepG-2 cells co-cultured with HL-7702 cells, and MCF-7 cells co-cultured with HEK-293 cells, as well as the combined fluorescence intensity of mitochondria and nucleoli calculated by confocal software for the different cell types (λex/λem of 488 nm/510–620 nm). Scale bars: 50 µm.

(a) Schematic of fluorescence intensity at different sites of the cell. (b) Fluorescence images of HepG-2 cells before and after stimulation in situ with 4 µM CCCP for 10 min following incubation with CDs (40 µg mL−1) for 1 h, and fluorescence images of normal HL-7702 cells incubated with CDs (40 µg mL−1) for 1 h (λex/λem of 488 nm/510–620 nm). Scale bars: 25 µm. (c) Separate and combined fluorescence intensity of mitochondria and nucleoli calculated by confocal software in healthy and damaged cancer cells and normal cells incubated with CDs (40 µg mL−1), as well as the ratio between respective cells.
Synthesis pathways for the CDs, and the principle of multiple-parameter-based discrimination between cancer cells and normal cells.

head.ce:title: Resolving variable cell viability-induced false negative: Accurate and high-contrast fluorescence diagnosis of cancer enabled by dual organelle targeting and multiple microenvironmental parameters responsive versatile carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Institute of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Institute of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Institute of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing effective fluorescence-based diagnostic methods for cancer is of great importance but remains a major challenge owing to the low contrast and limited accuracy induced by the variable cell viability. To increase contrast and accuracy in cancer diagnosis, a dual-organelle-targeting and multiple-parameter-responsive design strategy is proposed here. As a proof of concept, versatile carbon dots (CDs) targeting mitochondria and nucleoli with ability to respond to three environmental parameters (mitochondrial membrane potential, viscosity, and RNA) are developed. These capabilities not only enable high-contrast and accurate imaging of cancer cells but also make the CDs highly robust against interference from environmental parameters alterations induced by cell damage or apoptosis. This new design strategy involving targeting of multiple organelles and multiple responsiveness to environmental parameters could inspire further research in bioactive material-based cancer imaging.

Here, versatile carbon dots (CDs) targeting mitochondria and nucleoli with ability to respond to three environmental parameters (mitochondrial membrane potential, viscosity, and RNA) were successfully prepared to resolve the variable cell viability-induced false negative, which can realize accurate and high-contrast fluorescence diagnosis of cancer.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is one of the most serious diseases that threaten human health [1–3]. Cure rates and survival times can be greatly improved if cancers are found in the early stages [4–6]. Therefore, the early diagnosis of cancer is of great significance to human health and social development [7–10]. Cancer cells can be distinguished from normal cells not only by changes in morphology [11] and specific biochemical indicators [12,13], but also by the changes in various microenvironment parameters inside the cells including pH, viscosity, polarity and oxygen content [14]. Pathophysiological microenvironment parameters exhibit changes in the early stages of cancers and vary with cancer development, which can serve as tumor biomarkers and recently attract increasing attentions in the field of cancer diagnosis [15].

Human cells contain multiple organelles, and the microenvironment parameters of these organelles show a high degree of heterogeneity. Therefore, targeting specific organelles is required to achieve high accuracy in cancer diagnosis [16]. Compared to normal cells, the mitochondria and nucleus in cancer cells have many unique properties that are closely related to cell metabolism and bioenergetics. For mitochondria, the mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) and viscosity are two important microenvironment parameters used for evaluating the mitochondrial function and the cell health. Due to the high energy requirements, the MMP of cancer cells is at least 60 mV higher than that of normal cells [17,18], which can attract positively charged species to mainly focus in mitochondria. In addition, the mitochondrial viscosity in cancer cells is also higher than that in normal cells [19], which is probably attributed to the higher enrichment of lactic acid, proteins, and lipids [15]. For nucleus, the nucleolus is the most prominent substructure in the nucleus, which is mainly composed of RNA and is responsible for the production of ribosomes for protein synthesis [20]. Owing to their increased demand for protein synthesis, cancer cells produce more RNA and possess larger and more numerous nucleoli compared with normal cells [21]. Thus, with rational design and suitable signal output strategy, these features could be served as sensitive markers for cancer diagnosis.

Fluorescence-based imaging using fluorescent materials is of great promise due to its high spatiotemporal resolution, high sensitivity, simple operability and rapid response [22]. Carbon dots (CDs) [23], as a newly emerging fluorescent nanomaterial, have many attractive characteristics including good biocompatibility [24], low cytotoxicity [25], high photo-stability [26], and ease of synthesis and modification [27]. Therefore, they are among the most promising materials in the field of biological imaging [28–32]. Over the past decade, great efforts have been devoted to the development of fluorescent CDs for cancer diagnosis. Among them, fluorescent CDs for distinguishing cancer cells from normal cells based on MMP [33] and mitochondrial viscosity [34] or RNA have been reported [35]. However, CDs that respond to a single marker always cause insufficient accuracy due to the variable cell viability and high complexity of the human body. For example, the MMP decreases in damaged and dead cancer cells, resulting in the false negative in MMP-based cancer diagnosis [36]. Therefore, CDs with the ability to respond to multiple parameters, such as RNA, MMP, and viscosity, would enable to increase imaging contrast and improve accuracy in cancer diagnosis. Ideally, such CDs should meet the following requirements: (1) responding to multiple pathophysiological microenvironment parameters to increase the imaging contrast and accuracy for discrimination between cancer and normal cells; (2) targeting multiple organelles to reduce interference from changes in cell states induced by cell damage or apoptosis. To date, no such CDs for cancer diagnosis have been reported.

In this work, mitochondria and nucleolus dual-organelle-targeting versatile CDs were developed using 1-ethyl-2-methylquinolinium iodide and 2,4-dihydroxy benzaldehyde as precursors through a microwave-assisted thermal method. The CDs could respond to three environment parameters, MMP, viscosity, and RNA ( Scheme 1). These capabilities not only enabled high-contrast and accurate imaging of cancer cells but also endowed the CDs with high anti-interference ability against alterations in environmental parameters induced by cell damage or apoptosis. The fluorescence contrast ratio of cancer to normal cells was as high as six-fold, and still remained about four-fold when mitochondria were damaged, both of which are higher than the clinically acceptable threshold of 2 [37,38]. These versatile CDs therefore represent a promising tool for accurate cancer diagnosis.

Fifteen milligrams of 2,4-dihydroxy benzaldehyde and 10.8 mg 1-ethyl 2-methylquinolinium iodide (molar ratio 3:1) were added to a microwave tube with 5 mL ethanol as the solvent, and refluxed for 20 min at 130 ℃ with a microwave synthesizer. The product was purified by silica gel column chromatography (ethyl acetate: ethanol=1:1). A pure dark yellow solid was obtained after vacuum drying. The solid was dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide to form 2 mg mL−1 and 10 mg mL−1 storage solutions for later use.

The prepared CDs were diluted to 10 µg mL−1 with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), followed by addition of RNA solution (in CH3COOH/CH3COONa buffer, pH=5), to final concentrations of 20 µg mL−1, 40 µg mL−1, 60 µg mL−1. 1000 µg mL−1. Fluorescence emission spectra were collected in the wavelength range of 460–700 nm (440 nm was regarded as the optimal excitation wavelength, Fig. S2b).

Mixtures of PBS and glycerol were prepared at ratios of 0–100% by volume. The CDs storage solution was diluted to 10 µg mL−1 with mixtures containing different proportions of glycerol. Fluorescence emission spectra were collected in the 460–700 nm wavelength range (excitation wavelength=440 nm).

The HeLa cells were cultured with DMEM (10% fetal bovine serum [FBS], 0.1% penicillin-streptomycin) in a cell incubator at 37 ℃ and 5% CO2 for 24 h, then incubated with 40 µg mL−1 CDs for 1 h, and stained with MTDR (Mito Tracker™ Deep Red, 50 nM) and Hoechst 33342 (5 μg mL−1) for 20 min. Fluorescence images of cells were captured immediately without washing, using a Leica laser confocal microscope. Experiments were repeated three times with the same parameter settings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging of cancer cells and normal cells
ce:section.ce:para: Various cell types (HeLa, HepG-2, MCF-7, HT-1080, HEK-293, and HL-7702) were inoculated simultaneously into an 8 × 8-cm round cell culture dish. Cells were cultured with DMEM (10% FBS, 0.1% penicillin-streptomycin) in a cell incubator at 37 ℃ and 5% CO2 for 24 h, then incubated with 40 µg mL−1 CDs for 1 h, and imaging experiment was carried out immediately without washing using a Leica laser confocal microscope. Experiments were repeated three times with the same parameter settings. Further, cancer cells and normal cells were inoculated simultaneously into an 8 × 8-cm round cell culture dish then cultured, incubated, and imaged in the same way.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging of cancer cells after CCCP and LPS stimulation
ce:section.ce:para: HepG-2 and HeLa cells were cultured with DMEM (10% FBS, 0.1% penicillin-streptomycin) in a cell incubator at 37 ℃ and 5% CO2 for 24 h. For CCCP stimulation, HepG-2 cells were incubated with 40 µg mL−1 CDs for 1 h, followed by imaging with a Leica laser confocal microscope before and after 4 µM CCCP treatment in situ for 10 min. For LPS stimulation, HepG-2 cells were incubated with CDs for 1 h, then fluorescence images were captured with a Leica laser confocal microscope every 10 min after stimulation in situ with 4 µg mL−1 LPS. Further, HeLa cells were incubated and imaged in the same way.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging of HeLa cells after fixed and DNase, RNase treated
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cells were cultured with DMEM (10% FBS, 0.1% penicillin-streptomycin) in a cell incubator at 37 ℃ and 5% CO2 for 24 h, then fixed with 1 mL −20 ℃ CH3OH for 20 min. Later, the CH3OH was replaced with DMEM. Cells were treated with 300 µg mL−1 DNase or 300 µg mL−1 RNase for 2 h followed by incubation with 40 µg mL−1 CDs for 1 h. Then, imaging was performed immediately with a Leica laser confocal microscope without washing the cell dish. Experiments were repeated three times with the same parameter settings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design, synthesis and characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Given the electronegativity of the RNA surface and the negative MMP, positively charged 1-ethyl-2-methylquinolinium iodide was selected as a precursor to achieve the dual targeting of mitochondria and nucleoli through a mechanism of electrostatic interactions. 2,4-dihydroxy benzaldehyde with two hydroxyl groups was selected as another precursor to form carbon cores. A CC bond was expected to be formed by the Knoevenagel condensation reaction between the aldehyde group of 2,4-dihydroxy benzaldehyde and active methyl group of 1-ethyl-2-methylquinolinium iodide, which would rotate freely at low viscosity but be restricted at high viscosity, resulting in responsiveness to viscosity. Thus, CDs targeting mitochondria and nucleoli with MMP, viscosity, and RNA response abilities could be achieved (Scheme 1).

The CDs were synthesized by a microwave-assisted thermal method and the reaction conditions including temperature, feeding ratio of precursors, and reaction time were optimized. Since there is no fluorescence emission from the reactants but strong emission from the products under 440 nm excitation, the change of fluorescence intensity of reaction solution can be used to evaluate the reaction efficiency and screen the reaction conditions. As shown in Fig. S1, with the prolonged reaction time as well as the increased feeding ratio of 1-ethyl-2-methylquinolinium iodide and reaction time, the reaction efficiency rose initially and then decreased. We speculate that the fluorescence of the CDs synthesized may be mainly controlled by the mechanism of the surface molecular state emission [39,40], that is, small fluorophores are firstly formed at the early stage of reaction, followed by dehydration reaction to form the carbon core with increasing carbonization temperature and time [41,42]. Therefore, 20 min of the reaction time, 130 ℃ of the reaction temperature and a molar ratio 1:3 of 1-ethyl-2-methylquinolinium iodide to 2,4-dihydroxy benzaldehyde were selected as the optimal synthesis conditions and used in subsequent experiments.

The morphology of the as-prepared CDs was observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). It is apparent from the TEM imaging ( Fig. 1a) that the spherical CDs are uniform distribution with a 3.5 ± 0.5 nm diameter and a clear crystal lattice structure. Then, the presence of the chemical bonds was deduced by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum. The peaks at 3600–3200 cm−1, 2930 cm−1, 1600 cm−1, 1580–1450 cm−1, 1210 cm−1 and 1085 cm−1 in FT-IR spectrum can be ascribed to the stretching vibration of OH, CH, CC, benzene ring, CO and CN bonds, respectively, among which the existence of CC bonds confirm the occurrence of Knoevenagel condensation reaction between the two precursors and endowed the CDs with viscosity responsive properties (Fig. 1b). Next, the elements and chemical bonds inside the CDs were further characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). It could be seen from Fig. 1c that the main elements inside the CDs are C, N, and O, indicating that both precursors have been mixed in the CDs. There is a small amount of element I, which should be from the 1-ethyl-2-methylquinolinium iodide. The fine spectra of C1s (Fig. 1d) exhibit three peaks, indicating the existence of CC/CC (284.0 eV), CN/CO (285.0 eV), and CN/CO (288.4 eV) bonds. The spectra of N1s region (Fig. 1e) show three peaks centered at 399.0 eV, 400.1 eV, and 402.8 eV, demonstrating the presence of CN, CN, and cationic N bonds. The peaks in the fine spectra of O1s (Fig. 1f) are associated with COC (532.12 eV) and CCO (533.66 eV) bonds. These above results are in accordance with the FT-IR results. Additionally, to realize the enrichment in mitochondria and nucleoli, the CDs need to be positively charged after synthesis. Therefore, the zeta potential analysis was carried out. As can be seen from the Fig. S3a, the synthesized CDs were verified to be positively charged, which was about +5.8 mV, in agreement with our design.

The UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra (Fig. S2a) of CDs show two absorption peaks at 440 nm and 500 nm, and a large Stokes shift of 110 nm between the optimum excitation wavelength of 440 nm and the emission wavelength of 550 nm. To verify whether the CDs were generated by a single precursor or by the two precursors, reactions under the same conditions in the presence of only one precursor were carried out. As shown in Fig. S2b, the precursor 1-ethyl-2-methylquinolinium iodide or 2,4-dihydroxy benzaldehyde can only form blue (λem = 500 nm) or green (λem = 520 nm) fluorescent substance respectively, while the combination of them can form CDs with a longer yellow fluorescent emission wavelength (λem = 550 nm). These results further confirm the successful synthesis of novel fluorescent CDs. When the excitation wavelength is changed from 400 to 450 nm, the emission peak of CDs shows nearly no shift ( Fig. 2c). It has been reported that the excitation-independent emission characteristics should be attributed to the uniform microstructure and surface chemical states of the CDs [43–46]. Therefore, this result indicates that the CDs have a relatively homogenous surface states and can further validate our speculation of the formation mechanism of the CDs. Additionally, the CDs exhibit good solubility in PBS buffer solution at concentrations under 25 µg mL−1 (Fig. S2d, e).

Next, the optical sensing properties of CDs were explored. As mentioned in the preceding text, the CDs are positively charged, so we speculate that they should be able to bind to negatively-charged RNA by electrostatic interaction. With increasing concentration of RNA from 0 to 1000 µg mL−1, a progressive enhancement of fluorescence intensity at 550 nm was observed (Fig. 2a), which further validates our conjecture. The enhanced fluorescence was significantly quenched after addition of 100 µg mL−1 of an RNA hydrolytic enzyme (RNase). Additionally, when the CDs were treated only with RNase (without RNA), there was no significant change in fluorescence, which still overlapped with the pervious spectrum (Fig. 2b). These results indicate that the RNase itself does not affect the fluorescence of the CDs, and the fluorescence enhancement indeed results from the interaction with RNA, further demonstrating the fluorescence responsiveness of CDs to RNA. The enhanced fluorescence of CDs can be explained as follows: CDs were embedded in the hydrophobic grooves of the helical structure of RNA so that the rotation of the internal CC bond was inhibited and the non-radiative transition of CDs was reduced dramatically [47–49]. According to our design, the CDs need to be responsive to viscosity to achieve optimal cancer cell discrimination. Therefore, we tested the effects of different viscosities on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs in PBS-glycerol systems, where the viscosity of the solution was adjusted with different volume fractions of glycerol. As shown in the Fig. 2c, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs increases gradually in keeping with the viscosity enhancement, showing a strong linear relationship in the range of 0.893–945 cP (the viscosity of the tested solution was measured by viscometer). Moreover, the fluorescence intensity in pure glycerol is 60 times higher than that in PBS. At last, the CDs maintains steady fluorescence intensity both before and after the response to RNA or viscosity under continuous irradiation with excitation light for 1 h, indicating their excellent photostability (Fig. S2f).

As the cell is a complex microenvironment, we had to take into account possible interference from cations, anions, amino acids, reactive oxygen species (ROS), reactive sulfur species (RSS), and nucleic acids, which commonly exist in cells. It is evident from Fig. 2d that the CDs were insensitive to other substances except for two nucleic acids. Possibly due to the electronegativity of the DNA, it could also turn on the fluorescence of the CDs. However, it is worth noting that the DNA we used in the solvent system is oligonucleotide with only primary structure, while inside the nucleus of a cell, DNA is finely organized and associated with proteins to fold into a complex three-dimensional structure referred to as chromatin. Therefore, in the nucleus, the CDs are more likely to bind to RNA and enriched in the nucleoli, thus lighting up the nucleoli. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity at 550 nm of CDs in Britton-Robinson buffer solutions with different pH in the range of 2–12 was determined and the results showed that the CDs were insensitive to pH value (Fig. S3b). Collectively, all of these results demonstrate that even in the complex microenvironment of the cell with various interfering factors, the CDs could selectively respond to viscosity and RNA.

Before the CDs were used for high-fluorescence labeling of cancer cells, the optimal incubation conditions of cells were determined. First, the cytotoxicity of the CDs at different concentrations was performed on cancer cells (HeLa and HepG-2) and normal cells (HEK-293 and HL-7702) by MTT assay. Even at a high concentration of 60 µg mL−1 (Fig. S4c), the survival rate for all cell types co-incubated with CDs for 24 h remained above 80%, which proved that the CDs had very low cytotoxicity. Finally, 40 µg mL−1 was selected as the incubation concentration for subsequent experiments. We recorded the cellular uptake process of CDs into HeLa cells with confocal microscopy. At the beginning of incubation, CDs were mainly concentrated at the edge of the cell membrane, suggesting that CDs entered cells from the outside through the cell membrane. After 20 min, fluorescence was visible in the mitochondria and nucleolus, and gradually brightened over time, whereas that at the cell membrane edge dimmed. After about 60 min, the fluorescence intensity was no longer changed significantly (Fig. S4a), which was consistent with the time-dependent fluorescence change of the whole cells determined by the confocal data processing software (Fig. S4b). Of note, when calculating the fluorescence intensity of whole cells, at least three cells were selected and the mean and relative standard deviation values were calculated. Therefore, 1 h was selected as the incubation time for subsequent experiments. As shown in Fig. S5, when cells were imaged immediately without washing after incubation, all the fluorescence was concentrated in the cells, whereas the background fluorescence was almost zero. After washing with PBS buffer solution three times, the fluorescence intensity did not change significantly compared with that before washing. Therefore, in subsequent experiments, the cells were imaged directly after incubation with CDs without washing.

Co-localization experiments were carried out to investigate the organelle-targeting ability of the CDs in HeLa cells by using the commercial mitochondrial tracker (MTDR) and nucleus tracker (Hoechst 33342). Cell images were acquired with three fluorescence channels of yellow (λex/λem of 488/510–620 nm, Fig. 3a), red (λex/λem of 638/650–700 nm, Fig. 3b) and blue (λex/λem of 405/420–470 nm, Fig. 3c), corresponding to the distribution of the CDs, MTDR, and Hoechst 33342 in cells, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3e, the yellow (CDs) and red (MTDR) channels are highly overlapped and the linear shapes of mitochondrial are clearly observed, which indicates that the CDs could be well enriched in mitochondria. Moreover, as seen from Fig. 3f, the fluorescence region stained by CDs is the small spheres in the nucleus instead of the whole nucleus, indicating that the CDs can also selectively targeted the nucleolus within the nucleus. The nucleolus-targeting capability of CDs are probably attributed to the fact that the nucleolus is mainly composed of RNA, which is consistent with the RNA-responsiveness of CDs shown by the in vitro spectroscopic assays (Fig. 2a, b). Fig. 3g shows the calculated fluorescence distribution at different distances along a randomly drawn line in Fig. 3d. The fluorescence profile of the CDs was almost totally consistent with that of MTDR before 10 µm, whereas Hoechst 33342 had almost no fluorescence within this range. After 10 µm, MTDR had no fluorescence, whereas the fluorescence profile of CDs was partially consistent with that of Hoechst 33342. These fluorescence distribution results were in accordance with the above cell images, further confirming that the CDs were primarily enriched in the subcellular structures of mitochondria and nucleolus.

We also designed an enzymatic hydrolysis experiment with fixed cells to explore the selectivity of CDs between DNA and RNA at the cellular level. As shown in Fig. S6, when the cells that incubated with CDs were treated with 300 µg mL−1 DNase or RNase, their fluorescence intensity decreased by15% and 31%, respectively. In combination with the previous in vitro spectroscopy tests (Fig. 2a, b), it is clear that although DNA may interfere with the response of CDs to RNA in the in vitro spectroscopy tests, the CDs exhibit better selectivity for RNA at the cellular level. Additionally, to further prove the universality of the CDs for mitochondrial and nucleolus targeting, we performed the co-localization experiments in two other cancer cell lines (HepG-2 and MCF-7) and two normal cell lines (HEK-293 and HL-7702) (Fig. S7). The results were consistent with those obtained in HeLa cells, which can demonstrate that the CDs we designed and synthesized were suitable for mitochondrial and nucleolus dual targeting in multiple cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: High contrast imaging of cancer and normal cells
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the specificity of the MMP, mitochondrial viscosity and nucleoli in cancer cells, the CDs we designed with dual organelle targeting ability can be more easily enriched in cancer cells, thereby resulting in a much higher content of CDs in cancer cells. When we stimulated the normal cells and cancer cells with LPS that can induce an increase of intracellular mitochondrial viscosity, we found that the brightness of all cell lines increased gradually with time, indicating that the CDs can also successfully respond to viscosity at the cellular level (Fig. S8). Based on these, the viscosity and RNA responsiveness of CDs can further endow them with stronger fluorescence signal in cancer cells. Therefore, we speculated that the CDs we designed should enable high-contrast distinction between cancer cells and normal ones. To verify this speculation, four cancer cells (HeLa, HepG-2, MCF-7, HT-1080) and two normal cells (HEK-293 and HL-7702) were used to perform the fluorescence imaging experiments. As shown in Fig. 4b, after incubating with the CDs for 1 h, all the cancer cells were significantly brighter than normal cells when visualized, which was consistent with our expectations. Next, we selected at least three cells from each image and calculated the fluorescence intensities of mitochondria, nucleolus and the sum of fluorescence of mitochondrial and nucleolus (as described by the schematic in Fig. 4a) of all cell types. In all cases, the fluorescence intensity of cancer cells was much higher than that of normal cells, with a fluorescence contrast up to 3-fold. Furthermore, when the fluorescence intensities of mitochondria and nucleoli were summed, the fluorescence contrast ratio increased to almost 5-fold (Fig. 4c). To further explore the practical application potential of CDs in distinguishing the cancer and normal cells, we inoculated cancer cells and normal cells into the same Petri dish and co-incubated them with CDs. As seen from Fig. 4d, the fluorescence of cancer cells in each experimental group were significantly brighter than that of normal cells (HepG-2 vs. HL-7702, MCF-7 vs. HEK-293) with a high degree of discrimination. More significantly, we calculated the sum of fluorescence intensity of mitochondria and nucleoli, and found that the fluorescence signal of cancer cells was around 6-fold higher than that of normal cells, which far exceeds the acceptable clinical threshold of 2 (fluorescence ratio of tumor/normal tissues) [37,38]. Interestingly, the discrimination contrast of the co-cultured cells appears to be higher than that of two kinds of cells cultured separately. This may be because the metabolic rate of cancer cells is much higher than that of normal ones, leading to more efficient uptake of substances by cancer cells. Especially when co-cultured with normal cells, this difference could be more pronounced than that when these two kinds of cells cultured alone, thus resulting in a higher discrepancy of the CDs enrichment effect in the co-cultured system [33]. This experiment phenomenon is actually helpful to clinical cancer diagnosis. Taken together, the CDs with viscosity and RNA responsiveness and dual-organelle targeting capabilities can indeed achieve higher contrast for recognition of cancer cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-parameter response leads to higher accuracy
ce:section.ce:para: In our design, we expected that the dual-organelle targeting properties of CDs could not only enhance the imaging contrast between cancer cells and normal cells, but also reduce the interference induced by changes in cell viability. It is reported that the mitochondrial morphology is closely related to cell viability [50]. When apoptosis occurs to cell, the MMP is depolarized and decreases significantly [51–53]. Therefore, to test the anti-interference ability of the CDs during the cancer cell detection, we simulated a situation where mitochondria were damaged. We chose a powerful uncoupler of mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation, CCCP, to induce the loss of MMP and initiate cellular apoptosis. Fluorescence images of HepG-2 cells before and after CCCP treatment were acquired. As shown in Fig. 5b, after drug stimulation, the linear shape of the intracellular mitochondria disappeared and began to blur, and the brightness was also noticeably reduced, which led to a significant decrease in fluorescence contrast with the normal cells. Then, as illustrated by the schematic in Fig. 5a, the individual and combined fluorescence intensities of mitochondria and nucleoli of the aforementioned cells were calculated. After CCCP stimulation, the decreased MMP cause a decrease in the localization effect of CDs, leading to their dispersion from mitochondria to cytoplasm which has a water content of about 80% and a much lower viscosity than the mitochondrial matrix. Owing to the loss of CDs in the mitochondria and the low viscosity in the cytoplasm, the fluorescence in mitochondria and cytoplasm will be weakened. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of mitochondria decreases by 56% compared with that before CCCP stimulation, and the fluorescence contrast ratio of cancer and normal cells decreases from 4.2 to 1.8 (Fig. 5c), which is lower than the clinically acceptable threshold of 2. However, the nucleolus is a relatively stable structure in the cell and can retain its spherical shape after mild stimulation [54]. It is clearly revealed that the fluorescence intensity of the nucleolus is almost unchanged and the fluorescence contrast ratio of cancer and normal cells remains at 3.7. Owing to the stable fluorescence in the nucleolus region, the sum of fluorescence intensity of nucleolus and mitochondria diminishes by only 30%, and the fluorescence contrast ratio of cancer and normal cells remains at 2.9, which is above the clinically acceptable threshold. This same phenomenon was observed in two other cell types, HeLa (cancer cell) and HEK-293 (normal cells) (Fig. S9) These results imply that when cells are in an undesirable state, or during cancer treatment process when mitochondria are damaged, cancer cells can still be distinguished from normal cells with high contrast based on the fluorescence signal of the nucleolus only. Additionally, even with the sum of fluorescence of mitochondria and nucleoli as the signal, the fluorescence contrast can also exceed the clinical threshold. Consequently, as intended, the dual-organelle targeting of the CDs can efficiently enhance their anti-interference ability and thus improve the discrimination accuracy in cancer diagnosis.

In conclusion, to increase the contrast and accuracy of fluorescent imaging of cancer and normal cells, we have developed a new type of CDs with mitochondria and nucleolus dual-organelle-targeting ability and three microenvironment parameters responsiveness (MMP, viscosity, and RNA). These capabilities endowed the CDs with high anti-interference ability against alterations of microenvironmental parameters induced by cell damage or apoptosis. Most excitingly, the fluorescence imaging contrast ratio of cancer to normal cells could reach six-fold and remain above four-fold when mitochondria were damaged, which is higher than the clinically acceptable threshold of 2. These versatile CDs represent a promising tool for use in cancer diagnosis. The design strategy targeting multiple organelles with responsiveness to microenvironmental parameters could inspire further important research in cancer imaging.

The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. Zhaofan Wu: Data curation, Validation, Writing – original draft, Methodology. Zengqiang Liang: Validation, Formal analysis. Shuo Guo: Validation. Yue Xiao: Wring – review & editing. Yuanqiang Sun: Conceptualization, Supervision, Wring – review & editing. Lingbo Qu: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Zhaohui Li: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [21974125, 21708035]; Program for Innovative Research Team (in Science and Technology) in University of Henan Province (22TRTSTHN002); the 111 Project of Henan Province [CXJD2021001]; National 111 Project [D20003].

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zhaofan Wu is currently a MS candidate in pharmaceutical analytical chemistry under the supervision of Professor Zhaohui Li. Her research interests are focused on fluorescent carbon dots and their biological applications.

Zengqiang Liang has been studying for his Ph.D. degree in the College of Chemistry at Zhengzhou University since 2019, where his main research interests focus on design and synthesis of fluorescent probes and their biological applications.

Shuo Guo is currently a MS candidate in pharmaceutical analytical chemistry under the supervision of Professor Zhaohui Li. Her research interests are focused on fluorescent carbon dots and their biological applications.

Yue Xiao received his Ph.D. in Chemistry from the Institute of Chemical Biology and Nanomedicine of Hunan University in 2019. He is interested in developing new chemistries that would enable better, more biocompatible self-immolative polymers. Dr Xiao is currently a postdoctoral fellow at Zhengzhou University, working on the development of self-immolative polymer-based nanostructures for biosensing and drug delivery.

Yuanqiang Sun is an associate professor in the College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University. His research interests focus on the design and synthesis of fluorescent probes and their biological applications.
Lingbo Qu is a professor of College of Chemistry at Zhengzhou University. His research interests mainly focus on electrochemical and optical sensors.

Zhaohui Li received his Ph.D. from the State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics at Hunan University in 2007. He then spent four years as a postdoctoral fellow in the US, first at Tufts University and then at the Pacific Northwest National Laboratory. He is currently a full professor at Zhengzhou University, China. Dr Li is interested in nanomaterials for biosensing and drug delivery, and has published over 100 papers in related research fields.
